caveats: a: Occupation evidence is four-digit and includes other body/trailer industries.
a: The heterogeneous product mix widens the exposure range.
rho: No direct industry implementation survey exists.
rho: Recreational and utility manufacturers may have materially different digital maturity.

e: Different segments have different margins, making a single Auto Parts margin bridge fragile.

s5: Small private deals are incompletely reported.

q: Retention varies materially between branded RVs and commodity utility trailers.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Repeat-model product sales through dealers and distributors or direct fleet/customer orders, with model options, parts, warranty, and periodic product refreshes; end demand spans discretionary recreation and functional utility/light-trailer uses.
lens.deviation_from_default: The code combines recreational travel trailers/campers with functional utility, horse, automobile, and light-truck trailers and pickup caps. To keep the screen coherent, the lens retains repeat-model manufacturers with established dealer, distributor, or fleet channels across these products and excludes one-off custom fabrication; this narrowing is based on revenue-model coherence, not attractiveness.
lens.excluded_activities: One-off custom fabricators without repeat models or customers; truck semitrailers classified in 336212; mobile homes; repair and retail-only businesses; captive plants; and firms outside the EBITDA band.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market firms repeatedly manufacturing standardized or configured travel trailers, campers, pickup coaches and caps, automobile trailers, utility trailers, light-truck trailers, and single-car transporters for external dealers, distributors, fleets, and consumers.

a.bridge: BLS reports occupations for NAICS 336200 rather than 336214 and does not measure AI exposure. I assign partial exposure to dealer quoting, product configuration, design-document reuse, purchasing, schedules, marketing, compliance records, and warranty support while assigning little exposure to hands-on fabrication and assembly. The mix of standardized utility trailers and feature-rich RV products makes the aggregate direction ambiguous.
caveats: Occupation evidence is four-digit and includes other body/trailer industries.
The heterogeneous product mix widens the exposure range.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted 336214 occupation roster split between recreational and utility products, plus task-time samples for quoting, engineering, procurement, planning, marketing, quality, and warranty.
a.rationale: Production occupations are 65.21% of the broader industry's employment and assemblers/fabricators are 31.75%, so physical work dominates. Repeat models still generate substantial reusable information work around orders, options, materials, and dealer/customer service.

d5.bridge: Federal Reserve real production measures all 336214 output, RVIA wholesale shipments primarily illuminate recreational vehicles, and BEA RVing value added is a broader activity measure. I translate their post-boom contraction and modest shipment recovery into constant-quality lens demand; dealer restocking and the omission of utility-trailer end-use data can bias the estimate either way.
caveats: Wholesale shipments can reflect inventory rather than retail demand.
The production series aggregates disparate products.
Discretionary RV demand is highly sensitive to rates and consumer confidence.
d5.change_evidence: Segmented retail registrations, dealer inventory turns, orders and cancellations, used prices, financing approvals, utility-trailer fleet demand, and five-year unit forecasts for recreational versus work-oriented products.
d5.rationale: Real production was 81.2049 in 2025 versus a 2017 base of 100 after a pandemic-era boom and correction. RVIA reports 306,191 towable RV shipments in 2025, up about 2.5%, while its spring 2026 forecast centers total RV shipments near 349,000. BEA reports $27.5 billion of RVing value added in 2024. The base assumes broadly stable to modestly higher five-year demand, with wide consumer-cycle tails.

caveats: The provided n is margin-bridged and not observed.
Different segments have different margins, making a single Auto Parts margin bridge fragile.
e.change_evidence: Firm-level reconciliation of 336214 establishments to ultimate owners with normalized EBITDA, manufacturing revenue, repeat-model share, dealer/fleet recurrence, and product-segment tags.
e.rationale: The code includes many independent regional utility, specialty, camper, and trailer brands that can fit the LMM band and repeatedly sell through dealers or to external customers. Large consolidated RV plants, very small custom shops, captive production, and retail-heavy businesses reduce eligibility.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 336214 ($2,718,000,792) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 336214 ($30,741,107,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.0904 (margins.json[336]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Auto Parts EBITDA/Sales 9.04%); revenue/employee $464,325 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 336214); class <5: 167 firms, avg revenue/firm $623,216 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $56,339 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 93 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,660,323 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $150,093 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 100 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,781,970 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $341,890 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 143 firms, avg revenue/firm $11,397,636 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,030,346 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 63 firms, avg revenue/firm $48,439,873 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $4,378,965 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 26 firms, avg revenue/firm $977,801,885 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $88,393,290 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 206

caveats: Imports and consolidation can displace individual lens firms while physical operator demand persists.
o.change_evidence: Evidence that automated contract plants, imports, or dealer/private-label platforms fully eliminate independent domestic manufacturers across major product segments rather than changing their labor mix.
o.rationale: Trailers and campers are physical load-carrying or living-space products requiring fabrication, integration, inspection, warranty, and accountable delivery. AI can reduce information labor but cannot replace the manufacturing operator over five years.

caveats: PPI observes product selling prices rather than AI-benefit retention.
Retention varies materially between branded RVs and commodity utility trailers.
q.change_evidence: Dealer agreements and fleet/customer contracts showing rebates, floorplan support, rebid cadence, material escalators, promotional funding, option margins, and realized price/cost bridges after automation.
q.rationale: Brand, design, options, lead times, niche utility, and dealer availability allow retention, especially for specialty products. Standardized utility trailers and mainstream RV models face dealer bargaining, model-year discounts, price transparency, and many substitutes, causing substantial pass-through.

caveats: No direct industry implementation survey exists.
Recreational and utility manufacturers may have materially different digital maturity.
rho.change_evidence: Before-and-after evidence from multiple independent manufacturers showing AI adoption, labor hours, quote cycle, schedule adherence, warranty accuracy, rework, and integration cost for at least two model years.
rho.rationale: Repeat model libraries, option structures, dealer orders, and parts records support implementation within five years. Small-firm technology capacity, legacy ERP/MES, incomplete bills of material, seasonal staffing, product variation, physical quality control, and integration cost limit realization.

s5.bridge: Census ABS owner-age tables measure employer businesses generally rather than qualifying 336214 firms or control transfers. I use aging as succession pressure, then reduce for family succession, internal reorganizations, closures, minority deals, and failed sale processes; the relatively independent regional base may make the proxy directionally useful, but cyclicality can delay sales.
caveats: Owner age does not directly measure transfers.
Small private deals are incompletely reported.
s5.change_evidence: Five-year PitchBook, GF Data, Axial, and industry-association transaction data for US recreational and light/utility trailer manufacturers, tagged for control and reconciled to eligible firms.
s5.rationale: Founder-owned specialty brands and utility-trailer manufacturers can face succession, while consolidation creates buyers. Transfer execution still depends on dealer relationships, brand, warranty history, real estate, customer concentration, and cycle timing.

principal_driver: A broad independent specialty base and repeat dealer/configuration workflows create implementable information-work opportunities.
principal_weakness: The code mixes discretionary RVs with functional trailers, while most labor remains physical and the injected firm count is uncertain.
